summary: Martin Fowler provides an early preview of Microsoft's Oslo project, a Language Workbench announced at PDC 2008. Oslo consists of three components: M (a textual modeling language), Quadrant (a graphical design surface), and a repository for storing semantic models. Fowler is particularly impressed by the textual environment's approach of using compiled grammars that plug into text editors, maintaining true free-text editing rather than structured projectional editing. He notes Oslo occupies similar space to tools like Intentional Software and JetBrains MPS, but with interesting differences in its support for both textual and graphical DSLs. While cautiously optimistic, Fowler acknowledges Microsoft's track record of unfulfilled promises while seeing Oslo as potentially making DSLs much more prevalent.
key_points: Oslo provides a toolkit for defining new languages with three components: M for textual DSLs, Quadrant for graphical DSLs, and a SQL-accessible repository
The textual environment maintains true free-text editing unlike MPS's structured 'looks like text but isn't' approach, which Fowler has come to prefer
Microsoft originally intended only graphical tools but found they didn't work well for many problems, leading them to develop the textual components
The compiled grammar approach that plugs into text editors is praised as elegant compared to code generation approaches
Fowler wisely emphasizes using text files as authoritative source for version control rather than relying on shared repository collaboration
money_quotes: The marketing department will still use the same smarts that replaced 'Avalon and Indigo' with 'WPF and WCF'. I'm just hoping they'll rename 'Windows' to 'Windows Technology Foundation.'
When I first came across it, I rather liked the MPS notion of: 'it looks like text, but really it's a structured editor'. But recently I've begun to think that we lose a lot that way.
In the conversation Rebecca referred to this style as 'anecdotal testing' which is a phrase I must remember to steal.
The notion that we can all collaborate on a single shared database (the moral equivalent of a team editing one copy of its code on a shared drive) strikes me as close to irresponsible.
Code generation is all very well, but it always has this messy feel to it - and tends to lead to all sorts of ways to trip you up.
We do need to remember that all sorts of things were promised about Longhorn that never came to pass.
key_insight: Language workbenches that maintain true free-text editing while providing rich tooling through compiled grammars offer a promising middle ground between the flexibility of external DSLs and the tooling support of structured editors.
